title: podman expose a host device using `volume` option
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

/kind bug

**Description**
Using `-v` option, podman can expose a host device.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `podman run --it --rm -v /dev/video0:/dev/video0 fedora:36 /bin/bash`

**Describe the results you received:**
```
[root@02fbe52d6aa1 /]# stat /dev/video0
  File: /dev/video0
  Size: 0         	Blocks: 0          IO Block: 4096   character special file
Device: 0,5	Inode: 656         Links: 1     Device type: 81,0
Access: (0666/crw-rw-rw-)  Uid: (    0/    root)   Gid: (   39/   video)
Access: 2022-07-18 05:07:00.426449393 +0000
Modify: 2022-07-18 05:07:00.426449393 +0000
Change: 2022-07-18 05:07:14.058787863 +0000
 Birth: 2022-07-18 05:07:00.139449377 +0000
```

**Describe the results you expected:**
It should exit with an error.

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.1.1
API Version:  4.1.1
Go Version:   go1.18.3
```

comments.body: Why should it exit?  And how would it  know to exit?

Ok. You're right. It shouldn't exit. 

But I'm a little confused about the need of `--device` option.

What is the difference between `--device /dev/video0:/dev/video0` and `-v /dev/video0:/dev/video0`? 
From the code, I would say the options:
```
devMnt := spec.Mount{
	Destination: dst,
       Type:        define.TypeBind,
	Source:      src,
	Options:     []string{"slave", "nosuid", "noexec", perm, "rbind"},
}
```

@tupyy, `--device` has the ability to add CDI devices to the container config while I believe `-v` only mounts them as generic volumes in the config. End point has similar functionality, just different entities.

so basically, you can expose a device with `-v` option.
If this is the case, it needs to be documented cause it is really confusing.

I've been working on exposing the device with `play kube` commands but apparently it was not really necessary. I've could use `File` type in volume specs.

Device as root has some significant differences, in that it actually recreates the device node inside the container (as opposed to bind-mounting it in). As rootless, since we lack permission to do that, `-v` and `--device` are effectively identical.

Primary benefit of recreating would be that, after the initial run of the container, the major/minor number are baked into the spec, so changes to the device on the host shouldn't affect the container. Then again, that could be considered a negative, if you deliberately changed the device?
got it. Thanks @mheon for the detailed answer.
